# Mirodan PhD Vol 2 — Chapter IV: Applications (extract)

Source: /Users/opaque/Downloads/Mirodan-PhD-1997-Vol2.pdf, printed pp. 559-575
Extractor: parallel chapter agent
Status: complete
Page mapping verified: PDF page N corresponds to printed page N + 261 (Vol 2 starts at printed 262 = PDF 1). Chapter IV is at PDF pp. 298-314, printed pp. 559-575.

The chapter has two halves:
1. **Character Diagrams + Examples** (pp. 559-572) — one section per Inner Attitude (Adream, Near, Stable, Mobile, Remote, Awake), each with a 4-quadrant diagram (A/B/C/D) followed by character examples.
2. **Inner Attitudes and Dramatic Structure** (pp. 573-575) — the meta-pattern Yat Malmgren detects across plays.

OCR note: the PDF is scanned with OCR errors. "Adream" frequently mis-reads as "Adreaiu", "Adreazu", "Adream", "Adreain". "Iago" reads as "lago". "DSC" reads as "DEC" (Deciding), "ATT" sometimes as "All" or "ATI", "INT" sometimes as "Iflt". I have silently corrected obvious OCR slips in quotes; the underlying meaning is preserved.

---

## 1. Character Diagrams — structure / how to reconstruct

(pp. 559-572)

Yat Malmgren built one diagram-set per Inner Attitude (six attitudes total: Adream, Near, Stable, Mobile, Remote, Awake). Each set contains **four sub-diagrams labelled A, B, C, D**. They are "graphic form" reference cards "used as a quick reference tool when analysing a character and identifying the nature of its actions" (p. 559).

Mirodan states each diagram lets you see at a glance (p. 559):

> a. the Inner and Outer Character Drives (marked with a / in the text)
> b. the Action axis of Drives (marked with a - in the text)
> c. the different Variations opened to the character
> d. the Inner and Outer actions, with their respective 'linings'
> e. the Aspects and Stresses.

### Structural anatomy of a single sub-diagram

From the Adream set (p. 561) and its mirror sets, every sub-diagram is built the same way. Reconstruction recipe:

1. **Outer ring labels: the four Drives, placed at compass points** — `VISION` and `DOING` at top corners, `SPELL` and `PASSION` at bottom corners (or rotated by stress — see step 4). The Drive pair on the *vertical* axis is the **Action axis** of that diagram (e.g. "Spell-Doing", "Passion-Vision"). The Drive on top right reads as the dominant Drive, e.g. Adream "A" carries `SPELL/PASSION` (Spell over Passion) with a `Spell-Doing` Action axis.

2. **Inner ring: the two Outer Action Attitudes that bracket this diagram** — for Adream "A" these are `MOBILE` (Inner Action) and `STABLE` (Outer Action). The diagram is read as: dominant Inner Attitude (centre) covered by an Outer Action Attitude (visible behaviour) "lined" by an Inner Action Attitude (the secret driver beneath).

3. **Centre label: the Inner Attitude this whole diagram-set belongs to** (e.g. `ADREAM`).

4. **Bottom strip: the Stress + Aspect** — e.g. `SPACE-STRESSED ENCLOSING`, `SPACE-STRESSED RADIATING`, `TIME-STRESSED ENCLOSING`, `TIME-STRESSED RADIATING`. The four sub-diagrams A/B/C/D within an Inner Attitude exhaust the four Stress×Aspect combinations available to that Attitude.

5. **Side strip: the three Effort element pairs** that characterise the Working Actions of this Attitude, listed always in the same triple format. For Adream they are:
   - `STRONG/BOUND`
   - `LIGHT/FREE`
   - `STRONG/FREE` (and implicit `LIGHT/BOUND`).
   For Near: `STRONG/QUICK`, `LIGHT/SUSTAINED`, `STRONG/SUSTAINED`, `LIGHT/QUICK`.
   For Stable: `STRONG/DIRECT`, `LIGHT/FLEXIBLE`, `STRONG/FLEXIBLE`, `LIGHT/DIRECT`.
   For Mobile: `QUICK/BOUND`, `SUSTAINED/FREE`, `QUICK/FREE`, `SUSTAINED/BOUND`.
   For Remote: `DIRECT/BOUND`, `FLEXIBLE/FREE`, `DIRECT/FREE`, `FLEXIBLE/BOUND`.
   For Awake: `DIRECT/QUICK`, `FLEXIBLE/SUSTAINED`, `DIRECT/SUSTAINED`, `FLEXIBLE/QUICK`.
   These are the **two-element combinations** (the Action Drives, Effort × Effort) available within each Attitude. They are the practitioner's "vocabulary of moves" once you know the diagram you're in.

6. **Corner shorthand markers** — short codes inside each quadrant naming the two Action Drives meeting there (heavily OCR-mangled in the PDF; from context they are abbreviations like `INT/ATT` for Intending/Attending, `ADA/INT` for Adapting/Intending, `DEC/ADA` for Deciding/Adapting, `ATT/INT` for Attending/Intending). These pair the Working Action types — e.g. `INT/ATT` is the Stable corner; `DEC/ADA` is the Mobile corner; `ADA/INT` is the Adream corner; `ATT/DEC` is the Awake corner. The labels on each sub-diagram tell you *which Action Drives are foregrounded* in that variation.

### How the four sub-diagrams within a set differ

The four sub-diagrams of one Inner Attitude vary along **two axes**:

| | Stress | Aspect | Action axis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Space-stressed | Enclosing | Spell-Doing (for Adream); Doing-Spell (for Stable) — a "primary outward face" |
| **B** | Space-stressed | Radiating | Spell-Vision (Adream); Doing-Vision (Stable) |
| **C** | Time-stressed | Enclosing | Passion-Doing (Adream); Spell-Doing? — varies by Attitude |
| **D** | Time-stressed | Radiating | Passion-Vision (Adream) |

(For Stable the bottom row is Flow-stressed instead of Time-stressed; for Mobile/Remote/Awake the stress dimensions differ — Weight-stressed, Flow-stressed — see per-set listings below.)

### The six diagram sets, summarised

Each set: Inner Attitude → four (Stress × Aspect) sub-diagrams → each gives a Drive-pair Character + Action axis.

- **Adream set (p. 561)** — Stress dims: Space vs Time; Aspect dims: Enclosing vs Radiating.
  - A: Space-stressed Enclosing — Spell/Passion — Spell-Doing axis
  - B: Space-stressed Radiating — Spell/Passion — Spell-Vision axis
  - C: Time-stressed Enclosing — Passion/Spell — Passion-Doing axis
  - D: Time-stressed Radiating — Passion/Spell — Passion-Vision axis

- **Near set (p. 563)** — Stress dims: Space vs Flow; Aspect dims: Enclosing vs Circumscribing.
  - A: Space-stressed Circumscribing — Doing/Passion — Doing-Spell axis
  - B: Space-stressed Enclosing — Doing/Passion — (Vision-side variant)
  - C: Flow-stressed Enclosing — Passion/Doing — Passion-Spell axis
  - D: Flow-stressed (other Aspect) — Passion/Doing

- **Stable set (p. 565)** — Stress dims: Time vs Flow; Aspect dims: Enclosing vs Penetrating.
  - A: Time-stressed Enclosing — Doing/Spell — Doing-Passion axis
  - B: Time-stressed Penetrating — (Doing/Vision axis)
  - C: Flow-stressed Enclosing — Spell/Doing — Spell-Passion axis
  - D: Flow-stressed Penetrating — Spell-Vision axis

- **Mobile set (p. 567)** — Stress dims: Weight only; Aspect dims: Radiating vs Circumscribing.
  - A: Weight-stressed Circumscribing — Passion/Vision — Passion-Doing axis
  - B: Weight-stressed Radiating — Passion/Vision
  - C: Weight-stressed (other) — Vision/Passion
  - D: Weight-stressed (other) — Vision/Passion

- **Remote set (p. 569)** — Stress dims: Weight vs Time; Aspect dims: Penetrating vs Radiating.
  - A: Weight-stressed Penetrating — Spell/Vision — Spell-Doing axis
  - B: Weight-stressed Radiating — Spell/Vision
  - C: Time-stressed Penetrating — Vision/Spell
  - D: Time-stressed Radiating — Vision/Spell — Vision-Spell axis

- **Awake set (p. 572)** — Stress dims: Weight vs Flow; Aspect dims: Penetrating vs Circumscribing.
  - A: Weight-stressed Penetrating — Doing/Vision
  - B: Weight-stressed Circumscribing — Doing/Vision
  - C: Flow-stressed (one Aspect) — Vision/Doing
  - D: Flow-stressed Circumscribing — Vision/Doing

### The diagram serves as a "way in"

Mirodan emphasises (p. 559):

> The diagrams offer a quick route into the entire scheme of a character, regardless of the point of entry through which the actor arrives at it. Thus, if an actor starts with the Externalized Drives and defines his character as having, say, Spell and Passion, a glance at the diagrams will show an Adream character (Spell/Passion) and will give (Diagram "A") the rest of the analysis: enclosing Space-stressed, with an Outer Action of Stable (with a Near 'lining'), covering a Mobile Inner Action (with a Remote 'lining'). Finally, the diagram will show that the preferred Action axis of this character is Spell-Doing.

This is the meta-template: any single attribute (a Drive, a Stress, an Aspect) lets you locate the diagram and the diagram gives you everything else.

---

## 2. Mirodan's analytical method — procedural steps

Reverse-engineered from how she works each character:

### Step 1 — Locate the dominant Inner Attitude.
She names it first, often with one sentence of justification. Hamlet → radiating Adream. Lear → Passion/Spell (Adream "C"). Malvolio → Near. Hawthorne → Stable. The Inner Attitude answers "what does this character DO best, fundamentally?"

### Step 2 — Specify Stress and Aspect to pick the sub-diagram (A/B/C/D).
She always names both Stress (Space/Time/Flow/Weight) and Aspect (Enclosing/Radiating/Penetrating/Circumscribing) to pin down which of the four sub-diagrams applies. E.g. Claudius = "enclosing Space-stressed" → Adream A. Ophelia = "radiating Time-stressed" → Adream D.

### Step 3 — Read off Character Drives + Action axis.
Once the sub-diagram is fixed, the Drive pair (e.g. Spell/Passion) and the Action axis (e.g. Spell-Doing) come from the diagram itself. She rarely re-derives them; the diagram does the work.

### Step 4 — Specify Outer Action and its "lining" (Inner Action).
This is where the character's *visible* behaviour gets named: "Outer Action of Stable, with a Near lining, covering a Mobile Inner Action with a Remote lining." The lining is **the secret driver behind the visible Action**. Every Outer Action has a lining.

### Step 5 — Note polar visitations / fluctuations.
Mirodan repeatedly tracks **moments where a character travels to their polar opposite Attitude** and what triggers it:
- Creon "copes with a revolutionary situation" as Stable, but "at the end he breaks down into Mobility — his Flow-stress takes over" (p. 565).
- Olivia is "a Space-stressed, radiating Adream Character" who when "seized by this Remote state at her first encounter with Cesario, uses both Mobile-Stable and Awake-Adream Actions" (p. 570).
- Cordelia "starts as a Feeling type, but comes back at the head of an army: she starts as radiating Time-stressed, returns as a Passion/Spell time-stressed Character: Lear and her change places" (p. 561).
- Fortinbras: when Time-stressed Stable he uses Near and Awake Outers; when Flow-stressed Stable he can use Adream and Remote Outers (p. 565).

### Step 6 — Cite textual + production evidence.
Every reading is grounded in a play-text reference, a production memory, or a line. Examples: Mrs. Solness's "All the old portraits on the walls were burnt..." quoted as Near enumeration covering Remote (p. 571). St. Joan's "If I went into England against the will of God..." used as the proof of Awake mid-Near (p. 572).

### Step 7 — Hold readings in productive ambiguity when warranted.
She often names **multiple possible readings** and weighs them rather than forcing one: "Is Iago Near or Adream?" (p. 564); Zarastro could be Stable C or D — "I prefer the latter — the character becomes more flexible" (p. 566); Olivia in the Cesario scene "can therefore fit both 'A' and 'B' Remote diagrams" (p. 570); Olivier's vs Smoktunovsky's vs Finney's Hamlet are all valid (p. 574).

### Step 8 — Read characters relationally, not in isolation.
The Dramatic Structure section (pp. 573-575) treats the **whole cast as a system**. The character's Attitude is partly defined by which other characters it must contrast with. Iago's Inner Attitude "depends" on what Othello is — they are a polar pair.

---

## 3. Character analyses

Listed in the order Mirodan introduces them, grouped by the Inner Attitude diagram-set under which she discusses them.

### 3.1 Gertrude / Hamlet
- Inner Attitude: **Adream**
- Sub-diagram: **C** — enclosing, Time-stressed
- Drives: Passion/Spell, Action axis Passion-Doing
- Outer Action: Near; Inner Action ("lining"): Remote
- Reading (p. 561): "Her outer Action is Near, backed by the Remote idea of surrendering to Claudius's sensuality and presence so as to dominate still as the Queen."

### 3.2 Claudius / Hamlet
- Inner Attitude: **Adream**
- Sub-diagram: **A** — enclosing, Space-stressed
- Drives: Spell/Passion, Action axis Spell-Doing
- Outer/Inner: Stable outer with Mobile inner (the standard Adream "A" pairing)
- Reading (p. 561): "Claudius is an enclosing Space-stressed Adream Character with a Stable/Mobile outer (see above). He is Spell/Passion with a Spell-Doing Action."

### 3.3 Ophelia / Hamlet
- Inner Attitude: **Adream**, but barely Stable; she edges toward Mobile
- Sub-diagram: Adream **D** — radiating, Time-stressed; with Mobile incursions
- Drives: Passion/Spell, Action axis Passion-Vision
- Outer Action: predominantly Mobile lined with Near (Weight-stressed); the rare Stable moments are lined with Remote
- Reading (p. 561): "Her Intending has been totally annihilated by her submissive upbringing. Her last worldly prop (Nearness) is taken away when Laertes departs for Paris. She is left with a predominant Mobile outer Action 'lined' with Near (i.e. Weight-stressed — her desire for a Near relationship with Hamlet). She has almost no Doing — her Stability is very weak, and in the rare occasions when she has a Stable Outer Action it is 'lined' with Remote."
- Mobile-section restatement (p. 567): "Ophelia has no Weight so she clings to Weight outside herself. She seeks outer Nearness. She would like Stability, so she is looking for Weight. She is like ivy climbing up a tree."

### 3.4 Hamlet / Hamlet
- Inner Attitude: **Adream**
- Sub-diagram: **B** — radiating, Space-stressed
- Drives: Spell/Passion, Action axis Spell-Vision
- Reading (p. 561): "Hamlet is a radiating, Space-stressed Adream: Spell/Passion Character with a Spell-Vision Action (Diagram 'B'). Others like him in Shakespeare: Richard II, The Duke in Measure for Measure, Melancholy Jaques in 'As You Like It'."
- Note (p. 574): different actors choose different Hamlets. "Olivier's Hamlet was undoubtedly a radiating Adream type, but Smoktunovski and Finney chose to play strong, enclosing types."

### 3.5 Lear / King Lear
- Inner Attitude: **Adream**
- Sub-diagram: **C** — Passion/Spell (i.e. Time-stressed Enclosing)
- Reading (p. 561): "Lear is Passion/Spell (Diagram 'C')." Pairs with his daughter Cordelia's transformation.

### 3.6 Cordelia / King Lear
- Starts as Feeling type, **radiating Time-stressed** Adream (Diagram D)
- Returns as **Passion/Spell time-stressed** (Diagram C)
- Reading (p. 561): "Cordelia starts as a Feeling type, but comes back at the head of an army: she starts as radiating Time-stressed, returns as a Passion/Spell time-stressed Character: Lear and her change places, says Yat Malmgren."
- This is a worked example of the polar-flip principle.

### 3.7 Olivia / Twelfth Night
- Inner Attitude: **Adream** baseline, but spends much time in **Remote**
- Adream sub-diagram: **B** — radiating, Space-stressed (likes light-weight youths, attracted to Mobile outers)
- In Remote moments: Remote **A** — Weight-stressed Penetrating, Spell/Vision, Spell-Doing axis
- Reading (p. 562, 570): radiating Adream attracted to Mobile outers ("She, like Blanche du Bois, likes light weight youths"). On Remote, p. 570: "Olivia in 'Twelfth Night' has a lot of Remoteness. She is a great lady, but young — full, therefore, of her sense of being special, 'exclusive'. She adopts a mourning veil for seven years so as to defend herself from Orsino's overpowering sensuality."
- Cesario-scene fluctuation (p. 570): "When she sees young Cesario, Olivia tries to keep a Stable front, but inside she feels under attack (Mobile Inner Action) — her thought process is dominated by emotion. She 'thinks about emotion', like all characters in Remote states — she can therefore fit both 'A' and 'B' Remote diagrams in that scene. She is a Space-stressed, radiating Adream Character, who, seized by this Remote state at her first encounter with Cesario, uses both Mobile-Stable and Awake-Adream Actions. She is thus Spell/Vision, but with a Spell-Doing Action which turns at the end of the scene into Spell-Passion."

### 3.8 Brack / Hedda Gabler
- **Adream**, Diagram **A** — enclosing Space-Stressed, Spell-Doing (p. 562 and p. 575).

### 3.9 Hedda / Hedda Gabler
- **Adream**, Diagram **B** — radiating Space-stressed, Spell-Vision (p. 562, p. 575).

### 3.10 Lovborg / Hedda Gabler
- **Adream**, Diagram **D** — radiating Time-stressed, Passion-Vision (p. 562, p. 575).

### 3.11 Tesman / Hedda Gabler
- **Near**, Diagram **B** — Space-stressed Enclosing Near, Doing-Vision (p. 575). The Near "fourth element" challenging the Adream triangle.

### 3.12 Blanche du Bois / A Streetcar Named Desire
- **Adream → Mobile** (terminal flip)
- Adream sub-diagram: **D** — radiating Time-stressed, Passion-Vision (p. 562, 575)
- Mobile reading at the end (p. 567, Mobile B): "Blanche du Bois in 'A Streetcar Named Desire' finally 'evaporates' into Mobility: she is taken to a lunatic asylum. From the start we can see that she abhors Weight, she does not feel any attraction towards men, only towards boyish, androgynous youths. She is Weight-stressed radiating (aspires to sensuality but can not achieve it)/('B')."

### 3.13 Stella / Streetcar
- **Adream**, Diagram **A** — enclosing Space-stressed, Spell-Doing (p. 562, 575).

### 3.14 Stanley / Streetcar
- **Adream**, Diagram **C** — enclosing Time-stressed, Passion-Doing (p. 562, 575).

### 3.15 Mitch / Streetcar
- **Near**, Diagram **D** — enclosing Flow-stressed, Passion-Vision (p. 575).

### 3.16 Miss Julie / Miss Julie
- **Adream**, Diagram **B** — radiating Space-stressed, with Remote Outer Action; Spell-Vision axis (p. 562, 575). "Julie is longing for Nearness (peasant/servant warmth and passion)" (p. 575).

### 3.17 Jean / Miss Julie
- **Adream**, Diagram **C** — enclosing Time-stressed, with Near Outer Action; Passion-Doing axis (p. 562, 575). "Jean for Remoteness (aristocracy)."

### 3.18 Father / Miss Julie
- **Adream**, Diagram **C** — enclosing Time-stressed, Passion-Doing. "Jean and him share an inner nature, despite the class differences" (p. 562).

### 3.19 Christine / Miss Julie
- **Near**, Diagram **D** — enclosing Flow-stressed Near, Passion-Vision (p. 575).

### 3.20 Malvolio / Twelfth Night
- Inner Attitude: **Near** — and the most nuanced Near case
- Sub-diagrams: **B and C both apply** — he is "as Space-stressed as he is Flow-stressed"
- Reading (p. 563): "Malvolio is a perfect Near Character — he shows two sides of the Near coin. He is as Space-stressed as he is Flow-stressed. He is an ambitious hypocrite who would like to show himself as super-Stable: very reliable he is, after all, responsible for the welfare of a considerable estate. He also needs to show a lot of Awake — he can 'do' with ideas — he has a certain intellectual ability. In reality he reveals himself as circumscribing Near (terribly efficient). Behind the efficiency of Awake and Stable is a very sensuous Adream Inner Action, full of desires. We might imagine him in a 'private moment' dressing up as if he were Orsino with a dream of sensuous languor. Malvolio is different from Tartuffe, another hypocrite, who is Adream. Near is pedantic, exact. But Malvolio is only circumscribing in his outer behaviour, as a professional deformation, in his role as a steward, keeper of order and books. Underneath, he is ambitiously enclosing. Thus a very complex Character. (Diagrams 'B' and 'C' both apply to him)"

### 3.21 Sir Toby / Twelfth Night
- **Near**, Diagram **D** — enclosing Flow-stressed
- Reading (p. 563): "He drinks, belches, plans simple stratagems to gain possession of Olivia's wine-cellar — enclosing Near, Flow-stressed with an Adream outer, covering the Awake idea of marrying Olivia to Sir Andrew in order to achieve his own Stability ('D')."

### 3.22 Feste / Twelfth Night
- **Near**, Diagram **A** — Space-stressed (p. 563). Yat Malmgren note: "Near does not have to be low class."

### 3.23 Maria / Twelfth Night
- **Near**, Diagram **B** — Space-stressed (p. 563). High-class lady-in-waiting.

### 3.24 The Nurse / Romeo and Juliet
- "The most common type of Near"
- **Near**, Diagram **C** — circumscribing Flow-stressed, Passion-Spell (p. 563).

### 3.25 Rebecca Nurse / The Crucible
- **Near**, Diagram **B** — enclosing Flow-stressed, Passion-Vision
- Reading (p. 564): "She has warmth (Adream 'lining') but her outer Action is Stable (midwife — professional deformation)."

### 3.26 Iago / Othello
- Inner Attitude: **Near (open question)** — Mirodan flags ambiguity
- If Near: Diagram **A** — circumscribing, Space-stressed, Doing/Passion, Doing-Spell axis
- Reading (p. 564): "Is Iago Near or Adream? If Iago is Near, he is a very intelligent, circumscribing, Space-stressed, cool type: Doing/Passion ('A'). He plays games, does not really covet anybody sexually. His Action axis is Doing-Spell. Othello's is a sensuous jealousy, Iago's is a mean, calculating jealousy. Is his jealousy just professional or physical?"

### 3.27 Hawthorne (the judge) / The Crucible
- **Stable**, Diagram **A** — enclosing Stable
- Reading (p. 565): "He has the cool reserve and self confidence of the colonial establishment. He is enclosing Stable ('A'). If he were penetrating he would not be so stuck in his ways. He feels solid, lacks flexibility. He is predominantly Strong/Direct. He is Time-stressed more than Flow-stressed — he has no Adapting, remains always firm. Hence his outer Actions are Near and Awake. The Nearness is his strongest Attitude — mostly 'warm' — Strong/Sustained — out of a dangerous Sustained/Direct ('certain'): his working Actions are mostly Pressing and Gliding. His diagram is therefore 'A': Doing/Spell with a Doing-Passion Action."
- This is the longest worked example in the chapter and shows her full chain: Aspect → Stress → Outer Actions → "lining" → specific Working Actions (Pressing, Gliding) → Action axis.

### 3.28 Creon / Antigone
- **Stable**, Diagram **C** — copes with revolutionary situation; "At the end he breaks down into Mobility — his Flow-stress takes over" (p. 565).

### 3.29 Old Werle / The Wild Duck
- **Stable**, Diagram **A** — establishment figure (p. 565).

### 3.30 Fortinbras / Hamlet
- **Stable**; can be either Time-stressed or Flow-stressed
- Reading (p. 565): "As a General he gives the impression of having taken irrevocable decisions and of lacking any romantic attachments. He can, nevertheless, be Flow-stressed Stable and thus display Adream and Remote outer Actions. (When Stable is Time-stressed it acts through the more obvious Near and Awake outer Actions.) Being Flow-stressed Fortinbras can charm the Danish nobility with his Adream outer Action ('C'), while at the same time keeping them at bay with his Remoteness ('D')."

### 3.31 Dejaneira / The Women of Trachis (Sophocles)
- **Stable**, Diagram **B** — penetrating Time-stressed, Awake outer, Doing-Vision axis (p. 566).

### 3.32 Solness / The Master Builder; John-Gabriel Borkmann; Countess Rousillon (All's Well); Queen Margaret (Richard III)
- All **Stable**; Rousillon and Margaret could be played as Diagram **D** (p. 566).

### 3.33 Zarastro / The Magic Flute
- **Stable**, Diagram **C or D**
- Reading (p. 566): "enclosing, Flow-stressed Stable: Spell/Doing in Character and Spell-Passion in Action ('C'). But he could also be represented by diagram 'D': penetrating, Flow-stressed, Spell-Vision in Action. I prefer the latter — the character becomes more flexible."

### 3.34 The Captain / Heartbreak House (Shaw)
- **Stable**, Diagram **D** — "He 'stands for England' and her domination of the seas. He is an inventor — his Space is to the fore" (p. 566).

### 3.35 The Duke / Measure for Measure
- **Stable**, Diagram **D**
- Reading (p. 566): "fits diagram 'D': Flow-stressed, — Remote Outer Action with Near Inner. His objective has lots of Vision (to relieve the anxiety of death) while the Inner is full of Passion."
- (Note: at p. 561 Hamlet's group lists "The Duke in Measure for Measure" as similar to Hamlet — Adream radiating Space-stressed. So **the Duke admits two readings**: as Stable D from Stable-section perspective, as Adream B from Hamlet-grouping perspective. This is the kind of productive ambiguity she tolerates.)

### 3.36 The "Eighties Romanian Hamlet" courtiers
- **Mobile**, Diagram **A** — Weight-stressed circumscribing
- Reading (p. 567): "People who live under a tyranny are constantly Mobile — an entire nation can become Mobile. In the eighties' Romanian production of 'Hamlet', the guards during the first scene and the courtiers in 1,2, modelled on Ceausescu's entourage conveyed the palpable fear of the entire nation. They were Near, but their Mobile outer almost threw them off balance — Weight-stressed circumscribing ('A')."
- **Important**: Mirodan explicitly says (p. 567) "There are no genuinely Mobile characters, but there are extreme Feeling (Adream) types who lack Stability to the point where their Weight has gone almost completely." Mobile is **a state**, not a steady-state Inner Attitude for a person.

### 3.37 Viola / Twelfth Night (worked Mobile case)
- Base Attitude: **Adream radiating** (Vision/Passion, Space-stressed) — see p. 568.
- In the encounter with Olivia she **plays Mobile** Diagram **A** — Weight-stressed, Passion/Vision, Passion-Doing axis (p. 567).
- Reading (pp. 567-568): "It would therefore be wrong for her to bring Orsino's message playing an Adream action — after all, it is Orsino who is in love, not her. She therefore plays a Flow-stressed Near Action as in Mobile diagram 'A'. Besides, she is imitating her brother, her closest male role-model — itself a Near action. If we consider Viola as 'Mobile', she would be Weight-stressed and therefore Passion/Vision with a Passion-Doing Action axis ('A'). Her Inner Action is Remote — her love for Orsino, her grief for her lost brother and father. But, Viola is more intelligent than Sebastian. Sebastian is 'materialistic' in his approach to the world: he does not hesitate to flirt with the Captain, accepts his purse; the same thoughtless materialism lets him accept Olivia's hand so quickly. So, if we consider the twins Mobile (from Adream), Sebastian is more Passion/Vision (Weight-stressed — 'B') and Viola more Vision/Passion (Space-stressed). Diagram 'A' therefore fits Viola's behaviour in the scene with Olivia, when she pretends to be Weight-stressed, thus using a circumscribing Mobility to assume the role of 'suitor'."
- Sebastian: same scene-frame, Mobile **B** (Weight-stressed radiating, Passion/Vision).

### 3.38 Laura / The Glass Menagerie
- **Mobile** state — "She hates all things Near — hence her mother's idea of sending her to a typing course is absurd — there is nothing more Near than typing" (p. 568).

### 3.39 The Young Girl & Aunt / "Enemies" (Gorky), Mobile situation
- The encounter with hostile factory strikers is **a Mobile situation** (a state, not a Character):
  - Older woman: shock through **Adream** Outer Action ("B")
  - Young girl: shock through **Near** Outer Action ("A") — friendly Outer out of Inner relief
- Mirodan adds (p. 568): similar Mobile-situation examples in *The Wild Duck* ("thirteen at the table" exchange, end of Act I) and *The Crucible* (the interrogation of Elizabeth Proctor by Judge Danforth).

### 3.40 The "Man with the Mask" / Spring Awakening (Wedekind)
- **Remote**, Diagram **D** (Mobile Outer Action)
- Reading (p. 569): "He is absolutely Stable and as Flow-stressed as he is Time-stressed. Remoteness in him lies within his Objective: the idea of death to which both he and Melchior surrender creates absolute Free Flow and thus enables him to dominate, to be attractive, loveable, spell-binding (Mobile Outer Action — 'D')."

### 3.41 Christ-image as bridegroom / St. John of the Cross
- **Remote**, Diagram **B** — Adream outer with Stable lining
- Reading (p. 569): "St. John adopts a Remote attitude, having fallen under the spell of the Christ image — his outer Action is Adream (the love poems) with a Stable lining — the security of his faith, of his attachment ('B'). This creates a religious fervour bordering on hysteria."

### 3.42 Santa Teresa de Avila
- **Remote** state where mind dominates matter — example of physical pain not registering. Crosses snowy mountains with nuns; many die of pneumonia, she remains untouched (p. 569).

### 3.43 Ignatius de Loyola
- **Remote**, Diagram **C** — radiating Time-stressed Remote, with either Awake or Mobile Action Attitudes; Vision-Spell axis (p. 569).

### 3.44 Nero's Christians (collective)
- **Remote**, Diagram **A** — Weight-stressed Penetrating, Spell/Vision, Spell-Doing axis
- Reading (pp. 569-570): "the vows of the church take away Deciding and Intending: chastity is a denial of Intending; obedience means no Deciding; poverty means no material ballast — one is lifted above matter. Nero's Christians entered the Coliseum singing... The Christians displayed a Stable outer Action, behind which was the passionate Vision caused by the idea of eternal life (Mobile). They were destroying their fear and constructing their gesture with the idea of sacrifice. Theirs was a visionary Mobility, covered by a Stable Action (singing beautifully of love) coming out of a Remote state ('A')."

### 3.45 Yelena / Uncle Vanya
- Adream baseline; **enters Remote state** in Act II soliloquy "He's not in love with her..." Under the Remote Outer Action: Near attraction to the doctor, with Sonya as rival (p. 570).

### 3.46 Dona Rosita / Lorca
- **Remote** speech: "I have got used to living outside myself..." — Remote thought-process moves backward in time (p. 570).

### 3.47 Mrs. Solness / The Master Builder
- "**Tortured mind, Remote from Adream**", Diagram **D**
- Reading (pp. 570-571): "Mrs. Solness is a tortured mind, Remote from Adream. The Near Action then comes through — the Near connection to the details of the fire: 'All the old portraits on the walls were burnt. And so were all the old silk dresses. They'd been in the family for generations.' — is a good example of Near enumeration of fact and objects covering a Remote tortured mind. She then comes back to a Remote Outer Action with her talk of dolls — symbolic of the children who were burnt. Her Remote Outer Action is Vision-Passion ('D')."
- This is the **clearest demonstration** of what "lining" means in practice: visible Near specificity (lists of objects) on top of Remote inner state.

### 3.48 St. Joan / Saint Joan (Shaw)
- Baseline **Near**; uses **Awake** thought-process in argumentative moments
- Diagram: Awake **B** (Weight-stressed Circumscribing)
- Quoted line (p. 572): "If I went into England against the will of God, to conquer England and try to live there and speak its language, the devil will enter into me and when I was old I should shudder to remember the wickednesses I did..."
- Reading: "She is Near, but at this point she uses an Awake thought process ('B')."

### 3.49 Brecht's "consenters" / Lehrstücke (Young Comrade in Measures Taken; Boy in He Who Says Yes)
- **Awake at the moment of sacrifice**, Diagram **D**
- Reading (p. 572): "Yat Malmgren considers that acceptance (Job-like) is an Awake attitude. One could therefore argue that Brecht's 'consenters'... adopt an Awake Attitude at the moment of sacrifice, out of their Adream passionate affair with the Idea. Their Outer Actions become Mobile, quietly heroic and deeply moving, while the Inner Action is the Stability of the irrevocable decision ('D')."

---

## 4. Insights from the case studies — what we should apply to OUR character work

### 4.1 The diagram is the unit of character, not the Inner Attitude
Naming a character "Adream" is insufficient. The unit Mirodan/Malmgren actually use is **(Inner Attitude × Stress × Aspect × Outer Action × Lining × Action axis)**. That's six pieces. Every full reading hits all six. A useful character spec should produce all six.

### 4.2 "Lining" is the engine of psychological complexity
Visible behaviour (Outer Action) is *not* the character — it's the social face. The lining (the Inner Action that the Outer covers) is the secret driver. The most reusable craft technique in the whole chapter is the **Outer/Lining gap**: Mrs. Solness's catalogue of burnt silks (Near) covering a Remote tortured mind. Hawthorne's Pressing and Gliding (Doing) over a "dangerous Sustained/Direct ('certain')". Sir Toby's Adream outer covering an Awake plan. **For a voice/character spec, we want to design Outer + Lining as a pair, with the gap creating the tension.**

### 4.3 Mobile and Remote are STATES, not Characters
Mirodan is explicit (pp. 567, 569): "There are no genuinely Mobile characters" / "Like Mobile, Remote is impossible to achieve in the flesh." They are **transient states** a Stable/Adream/Near character visits under pressure. **Implication for any agent character:** never assign Mobile or Remote as the baseline. Reserve them for moments of overwhelm, religious ecstasy, terror, soliloquy-into-the-past, ego-disintegration. Use them as **failure-mode signatures** or **peak-state signatures**.

### 4.4 Polar flips drive narrative
Cordelia and Lear "change places". Creon "breaks down into Mobility" at the end. Blanche "evaporates into Mobility". Olivia's Cesario scene runs a real-time Stable→Mobile collapse of the Inner Action. **Voice-spec implication:** build in the polar opposite Attitude and a trigger condition that flips the character there briefly. That's where drama lives.

### 4.5 Productive ambiguity is the right answer, not the wrong one
"Is Iago Near or Adream?" is left open. Olivia "fits both 'A' and 'B'" in the Cesario scene. Malvolio is two diagrams at once (B + C). Zarastro: Mirodan explicitly prefers C-or-D for flexibility. **Don't lock the spec to one quadrant if a character genuinely lives between two — name the pair.**

### 4.6 Cast/system thinking
The Dramatic Structure section (pp. 573-575) is a cheat code: most plays distribute into **four 4-character clusters** (Adream-enclosing, Adream-radiating, Near-enclosing, Near-circumscribing). The core triangle is usually Adream; a Near "fourth element" gates or troubles it. **For multi-agent or multi-character work, place the cast across the Attitude-grid intentionally.**

### 4.7 The procedure: location → variant → outer-with-lining → axis → fluctuations → evidence
Six steps (Method §2 above). When you ask Mirodan to read a character, this is what falls out. **For our spec work**, follow the same march: Inner Attitude → Stress+Aspect → Outer Action+Lining → Action axis → polar visitations + triggers → textual citations.

### 4.8 The Working Actions are nameable
Hawthorne does Pressing and Gliding. That's the most concrete it gets — specific Effort combinations enacted on stage. **For voice work, name the 1-3 dominant Working Actions** (e.g. Pressing, Gliding, Floating, Wringing, Punching, Slashing, Dabbing, Flicking) the character defaults to. This gives an actor-direction-quality concreteness that abstract Drives never reach.

### 4.9 Stress dimensions vary by Attitude
Critical: Adream lives in Space×Time. Near lives in Space×Flow. Stable lives in Time×Flow. Mobile is Weight only. Remote is Weight×Time. Awake is Weight×Flow. **You can't ask "what's this character's Stress?" without knowing the Attitude first.** Different Attitudes have different stress menus.

### 4.10 Transformation in role
Cordelia, Blanche, Creon, Olivia all transform across the play. The Inner Attitude is **stable through the play** but the Stress, Aspect, Outer Action, and visited polar-states all evolve. **The character spec should encode trajectory, not snapshot.**

---

## 5. Surprises / overrides

### 5.1 Same character can occupy multiple diagrams
Malvolio is **both** Near B *and* Near C (p. 563) — one for his outer professional behaviour, one for his enclosing ambition. Olivia is **both** Remote A and Remote B in one scene (p. 570). Zarastro is **C or D** (p. 566). The framework permits — in fact requires — a character to be multiply located when their conflicts are real.

### 5.2 The Duke (Measure for Measure) is read inconsistently across the chapter
On p. 561 he is grouped with Hamlet as "radiating Adream" (Diagram B). On p. 566 he is read as "Stable D" with Remote Outer Action and Near Inner. **Two different sub-diagrams in two different sections.** Not flagged as an error — it's a feature: different scenes/moments support different readings.

### 5.3 Nationally-distributed Mobile
"An entire nation can become Mobile" (p. 567). The framework scales to crowd/group/cultural Inner Attitudes, not just individuals. Useful for any project where we treat a community or org as a "character".

### 5.4 Class assumptions inverted
"Yat Malmgren reminds his students that Near does not have to be low class" (p. 563). Maria, Feste, Curio, Valentine are all Near and high-class. Resist the cheap shorthand of mapping Near to "servants".

### 5.5 Acceptance = Awake
"Yat Malmgren considers that acceptance (Job-like) is an Awake attitude" (p. 572). Counter-intuitive: passive consent is reframed as a Vision-Doing alertness. Brecht's consenters at sacrifice are Awake D. **This redefines surrender as a form of clear-eyed action** rather than collapse.

### 5.6 Religious and ecstatic states have their own diagram
Remote isn't just "removed" — it's the Inner Attitude of Christian martyrs, mystics, saints, charismatic congregations, hysteria. The whole p. 569-570 stretch is a quiet override: Remote is a *spiritual* technology in the framework, not just an emotional withdrawal.

### 5.7 The "lining" can completely invert the surface
Sir Toby looks like a buffoon (Adream outer). Underneath: an Awake calculator, with Stability as goal. Hawthorne looks reliable (Near outer). Underneath: a Stable certainty bordering on the dangerous. Mrs. Solness lists silk dresses (Near specificity). Underneath: Remote tortured grief. **The lining usually contradicts or complicates the surface — that's the point.**

### 5.8 Stable characters tend to be solitary
"It would be difficult to find a Stable triangle or square, because it would be most unusual to have more than one Stable Character in a play" (p. 575). Stable is **structurally singular** — one Stable per cast. (Plays with two Stables would lack productive antagonism; the single Stable typically faces an Adream/Near constellation.)

### 5.9 The polar pair Electra/Clytemnestra
Footnote on p. 575: "Yat Malmgren makes a similar point about Electra and Clytemnestra — the oppressor has a secret longing to take the victim's place and vice-versa." The polar-flip is **mutual** and **secret**. Each pole *wants* its opposite.

### 5.10 OCR caveat
The PDF chapter has consistent OCR errors on Adream (often "Adreaiu"/"Adreazu"/"Adream"), Iago (always "lago"), and Effort-shorthand inside diagram quadrants. The structural information is intact; spelling slips don't change the readings.

---
